title: Name and describe your alert rule

blocks.content:
A clear, descriptive name is the first thing responders see when an alert fires. A good name tells them immediately what's wrong without needing to open the alert details.

In this guide, you'll navigate to the alert rule form and learn how to write effective names and descriptions for your alert rules.
You'll navigate to the alert rule creation form where you can define a new alert.
Navigate to **Alerts & IRM > Alerting > Alert rules** from the main menu.
Click **+ New alert rule** to open the alert rule creation form.
You're now on the alert rule creation form.
You'll enter a descriptive name for your alert rule that tells responders exactly what triggered it.

blocks.reftarget: input[placeholder='Give your alert rule a name']
blocks.content:
Enter a descriptive name in the **Alert rule name** field.

A good alert name clearly identifies what's being monitored and what condition triggers the alert.
blocks.tooltip: Responders see this name first when the alert fires.

blocks.content:
Use a naming pattern that includes the signal, the scope, and the condition:

| Pattern | Example |
|---------|--------|
| Signal + scope | `High CPU usage - Linux servers` |
| Signal + service | `Error rate spike - API gateway` |
| Signal + threshold | `Disk space critical - Database servers` |
| Log pattern + scope | `OOM errors - Production pods` |

Avoid vague names that don't help responders act quickly:

| Avoid | Prefer |
|-------|--------|
| `CPU alert` | `High CPU usage - Production web servers` |
| `Error alert` | `Error rate above 10/min - Payment service` |
| `Disk warning` | `Disk space below 10% - Database cluster` |
A descriptive name helps responders triage alerts quickly, especially when multiple alerts fire simultaneously.
You've learned how to write an effective alert rule name. A well-named alert tells responders what's wrong, where it's happening, and how urgent it is — all before they open the details.
